question: What equipment do you have on the bridge of your vessel?
ideal_answer: On my current vessel we have two radars — one X-band and one S-band. We have ECDIS as our primary means of navigation with a backup unit. AIS Class A transponder. Magnetic compass and gyro compass. Echo sounder. Speed log. VHF radios — DSC equipped. NAVTEX receiver. GPS as our primary position fixing system. Rate of turn indicator. Rudder angle indicator. Bridge Navigational Watch Alarm System. Sound reception system. And the required publications — chart catalogue, nautical almanac, tide tables, and the corrected paper charts as backup to ECDIS.
key_points: Radar — X-band and S-band, know the difference
ECDIS — primary navigation with backup arrangements
unacceptable_answers: Just listing two or three items
Not knowing what equipment is on their own vessel

examiner_preferences.style_notes: This is a warm-up question. Examiner wants to see the candidate is familiar with their own bridge. Lists are fine here but follow-ups will go deep on individual items.

follow_ups: What radar do you use for navigation and how do you set it up?
What are the disadvantages of ECDIS?
What are the disadvantages of AIS?
Who is required to have AIS fitted?
examiner_notes: From real exam: this was the opening technical question after 20min of casual career chat. Examiner then drilled into individual pieces of equipment. Know your own bridge cold.

question: What are the disadvantages of ECDIS?
ideal_answer: The main disadvantages are over-reliance on the system and complacency — it's easy to stop looking out the window. The display can give a false sense of accuracy — the position shown is only as good as the GPS input, and chart data may have survey limitations. Over-zooming can hide important information. Under-zooming can clutter the display. If the safety contour and safety depth are not set correctly for your vessel, you may not get appropriate alarms. ENC coverage is not complete worldwide — some areas still only have raster chart coverage. The system requires regular software and chart updates. And there's the risk of screen glare affecting night vision on the bridge.
key_points: Position accuracy depends on GPS input
Safety contour and safety depth settings critical
unacceptable_answers: Not understanding the difference between ENC and RNC

examiner_preferences.style_notes: Examiner wants depth of understanding about ECDIS limitations, not just advantages.

follow_ups: How do you set the safety contour and safety depth? How do you work it out?
Where would you find the variation on an ECDIS screen?
What happens if your GPS fails — what will stop working on the bridge?
examiner_notes: From real exam: examiner asked about ECDIS disadvantages, then showed a screenshot of ECDIS screen and asked where to find variation. Followed up with safety contour/depth configuration.

question: Your GPS fails. What equipment on the bridge will stop working, what are your actions, and how can you fix your position?
ideal_answer: If GPS fails, the ECDIS will lose its automatic position update — the displayed position will become unreliable or freeze. AIS will stop transmitting accurate position data. Any radar overlay on ECDIS will be affected. The speed log may still work independently but GPS-derived speed over ground will be lost. My immediate actions would be to inform the master, switch to manual position fixing, and increase the watch. For coastal navigation I would fix my position using radar ranges and bearings off charted objects, visual bearings using the compass and plotting on the paper chart backup, or if available use a secondary positioning system like LORAN or another GNSS constellation. I would also check if it's a receiver fault and try switching to the backup GPS if fitted.
key_points: Radar ranges and bearings off charted objects
unacceptable_answers: Not knowing what systems depend on GPS
No idea how to fix position without GPS
follow_ups: How would you use parallel indexing on radar to monitor your track?
What is the minimum number of position lines needed for a fix?
examiner_notes: From real exam: examiner wanted to see the candidate understand GPS dependency and have practical fallback skills. Connected to radar PI discussion.

question: Explain AIS — how does it work and what are its disadvantages? Who is required to have it?
ideal_answer: AIS is the Automatic Identification System. It's a VHF transponder that automatically transmits and receives vessel information on two dedicated VHF frequencies. It broadcasts static information like vessel name, MMSI, dimensions, and type. It also sends dynamic data — position, course over ground, speed over ground, heading, and rate of turn — which updates every few seconds depending on speed. SOLAS requires Class A AIS on all vessels over 300 gross tonnage on international voyages and all passenger ships. The disadvantages are that not all vessels carry it — small vessels, fishing boats, and warships may not have it or may switch it off. The data is only as accurate as what's been entered — often the navigation status, draught, or destination is wrong. And it should never replace radar or visual lookout for collision avoidance.
key_points: Static data: name, MMSI, dimensions, type
Dynamic data: position, COG, SOG, heading, ROT
SOLAS requirement: vessels over 300GT international, all passenger ships
Disadvantages: not all vessels carry it, data accuracy depends on input
Does not replace radar or visual lookout
unacceptable_answers: Using AIS as sole means of collision avoidance
examiner_notes: From real exam: examiner discussed AIS at length, particularly disadvantages and who must carry it. Practical understanding expected.

question: What is the significance of variation, and what about deviation — what causes it?
ideal_answer: Variation is the angular difference between true north and magnetic north at any given position on the earth. It changes depending on where you are geographically and it changes slowly over time — the annual rate of change is shown on the chart compass rose. Deviation is the error in the magnetic compass caused by the vessel's own magnetic field — the steel in the hull, engines, electrical equipment, and cargo. Deviation changes with the vessel's heading, which is why we have a deviation card that shows the error on different headings. The compass needs to be swung regularly to update this card, particularly after structural changes, loading magnetic cargo, or long periods in dock.
key_points: Variation: difference between true north and magnetic north
Annual rate of change on chart compass rose
Deviation: compass error from vessel's own magnetic field
Deviation card shows error per heading
Compass must be swung to update
unacceptable_answers: Not knowing when to swing the compass
examiner_notes: From real exam: this was asked early as a fundamentals check. Examiner wanted discussion about deviation causes specifically.

question: How do you use parallel indexing on radar for coastal navigation, and how can you determine you're on track?
ideal_answer: Parallel indexing is a radar technique where I set an electronic bearing line parallel to my planned track at a predetermined range from a fixed radar-conspicuous object — like a headland or lighthouse. As I navigate along my track, if the radar return from that object tracks along the PI line, I know I'm on my planned course. If it drifts above or below the line, I know I'm set off track and can make corrections. I set it up during passage planning by measuring the perpendicular distance from the reference object to my planned track on the chart, then setting the PI line at that range offset on the radar. It's particularly useful in poor visibility and at night when visual references are limited.
key_points: EBL set parallel to planned track
Object tracking along PI line means on track
unacceptable_answers: Not knowing what parallel indexing is
Confusing PI with radar plotting for collision avoidance
Not understanding how to set it up from the chart
follow_ups: Why not use a buoy for parallel indexing?
How often would you check your PI line during a coastal passage?
examiner_notes: From real exam: examiner asked about PI as follow-up to GPS failure scenario. Asked specifically why not use a buoy (answer: buoys can drag or be off station). High-value practical question.
